sections.recommendation:
Conduct a public hearing and, upon conclusion, adopt a Resolution affirming the Zoning
Adjustments Board (ZAB) decision to approve a Use Permit to demolish a gas station,
and construct a 10-story (114 feet) mixed-use building utilizing a State Density Bonus
request, with 166 dwellings, including 17 Very Low-Income units, and 1,000 square-feet
of commercial space, and dismiss the appeals.
SUMMARY
On May 11, 2023 ZAB approved the Use Permit for 3000 Shattuck Avenue authorizing
construction of the mixed-use multifamily building. The ZAB approval was appealed to the
City Council by three parties: a law firm representing Laborers International Union of North
America, Local Union 304; 34 people who own or lease property within 300 feet of the
project site; and a law firm representing East Bay Residents for Responsible Development.
The appellants have identified alleged issues with the environmental review of the project.
The City’s responses to appellants’ contentions are set forth below.
sections.current_situation:
A Use Permit application was submitted for this site in 2015 (ZP2015-0229). An Initial
Study-Negative Declaration (IS-ND) was prepared for a five-story project with 44
dwelling units, 17 off-street parking spaces, and 6,000 square feet of commercial space.
The project was revised by ZAB to 23 units, 6 parking spaces, and 3,522 square feet of
commercial space. The revised project was approved by Council, and the IS-ND was
adopted by Council on November 27, 2018. The project was not pursued.
On December 14, 2021, Isaiah Stackhouse submitted a complete Preliminary
Application to vest development rights for the proposed project at 3000 Shattuck
Avenue pursuant to Senate Bill (SB) 330, the Housing Crisis Act of 2019 (Government
Code Sections 65941.1 and 66300), for a housing development project.

On March 29, 2022, Isaiah Stackhouse submitted an application for a Use Permit
(ZP2022-0046) to construct a ten-story, mixed-use building with 166 dwelling units,
1,043 square feet of ground floor commercial space, and no off-street car parking
spaces, using a State Density Bonus.
On July 8, 2022, the applicant submitted a letter from their attorney requesting an
Addendum to the previous IS-ND for the five-story project, per CEQA Guidelines
Sections 15162 and 15164. On July 29, 2022 staff agreed to move forward with an
Addendum because the new project is not substantially different from the previous
project.
On August 3, 2022, the use permit application was deemed complete.
On January 27, 2023, an Addendum to the 2018 IS-ND was published.
On April 27, 2023, staff posted the public hearing notice of the ZAB hearing at the site
and six nearby locations, and mailed notices to property owners and oc
sections.background:
The project site is located on the southwest corner of Ashby Avenue and Shattuck
Avenue. The surrounding area consists of a mix of commercial and residential
development ranging from one to three stories in height. The Ashby BART station is 0.3
miles to the west. In addition, bus service is available via AC Transit routes 18 and 800.
The 14,000-square-foot project site comprises two parcels (Figure 1), and is currently
developed with a gas station that includes a mini-market with an office, car repair
services, smog test center, two gas station canopies with pump islands, and surface
parking for 11 automobiles (Figure 2). Site access to is provided from both Ashby and
Shattuck Avenues.
Figure 1: Zoning Map
Page 3
Page 4 of 668
ZAB Appeal: 3000 Shattuck Avenue PUBLIC HEARING
Use Permit #ZP2022-0046 March 25, 2025
Figure 2: Current conditions, facing southwest (Google Street View, 2024)
The proposed project would demolish the existing commercial buildings, remove the
existing paving and the curb cuts, and construct a new 10-story mixed-use building with:
 166 dwelling units (all studio units) for a total of 166 bedrooms;
 Commercial space: 1,043 square feet in area, for one tenant on the ground floor at
the northwest corner of the property;
 Two bicycle storage rooms on the ground floor with storage for 62 bicycles, and five
short-term bicycle parking spaces along Shattuck Avenue and Ashby Avenue;
 Residential access from Shattuck Avenue;
 New street trees along Shattuck Avenue and Ashby Avenue;
 Useable open space totaling 8,516 square feet consisting of ground level space and
a roof deck.
The project is eligible for a Density Bonus under Government Code Section 65915, by
providing 17 Very Low-Income units (15 percent of the base project) on site, and
qualifies for a 50 percent density bonus, or 56 bonus units, of which 55 are utilized,
resulting in a 166-unit density bonus project, with waivers to:
1) FAR. Exceed the Floor Area Ratio (FAR) limit from 4.0 to 6.
sections.rationale:
The issues raised in the appellants’ letters and staff’s responses follow. There are three
appeal letters to consider for this hearing and the appeal points are presented in the
order that the letters were received. For the sake of brevity, the appeal issues are not
1 The Housing Accountability Act (HAA), Government Code Section 65589.5(j), requires that when a
proposed housing development complies with the applicable, objective general plan and zoning
standards, but a local agency proposes to deny the project or approve it only if the density is reduced, the
agency must base its decision on written findings supported by substantial evidence that the development
would have a specific adverse impact on public health or safety unless disapproved, or approved at a
lower density; and there is no feasible method to satisfactorily mitigate or avoid the specific adverse
impact, other than the disapproval, or approval at a lower density.

Appeal Letter 1 was submitted by the law firm of Lozeau Drury LLP on behalf of
Laborers International Union of North America, Local Union 304 (“LiUNA”)
LiUNA Appeal - Issue 1: California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) compliance.
The Appellant asserts that the Addendum to the 2018 Initial Study-Negative
Declaration (IS-ND) was not appropriate for this review because new evidence
has been submitted and the IS-ND failed to consider it. (Appeal Letter 1,
Attachment 2, pages 1-2).
Response: CEQA Guidelines Section 15162 “Subsequent EIRs and Negative
Declarations” identifies three criteria that would require an updated document to be
prepared.
1. Substantial changes are proposed which will require major revisions of the
previous negative declaration due to new significant environmental effects or a

sections.environmental:
The project approved by the ZAB is found to be in compliance with all applicable State
and local environmental requirements, would be located in a transit-rich area, and would
be built and operated according to current codes for energy conservation, waste
reduction, low toxicity, and other factors.
sections.alternatives:
Pursuant to BMC Section 23.410.040(G), the Council may (1) continue the public
hearing, (2) reverse, affirm, or modify the ZAB’s decision, or (3) remand the matter to
the ZAB.
Action Deadline:
Pursuant to BMC Section 23.410.040(I), if the disposition of the appeal has not been
determined within 30 days from the date the public hearing was closed by the Council
(not including Council recess), then the decision of the Board shall be deemed affirmed
and the appeal shall be deemed denied.
